Name: curvature_aware_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing via Deterministic Spectral Proxies
Short Hypothesis: The spectral decay rate and Hessian condition number of hidden states provide deterministic proxies for quantization sensitivity, enabling training-free precision routing that optimizes computational efficiency while preserving generalization stability.
Related Work: Existing mixed-precision approaches rely on heuristic calibration or static profiling, which fail to adapt to dynamic input distributions. Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) introduces a training-free framework based on activation covariance geometry, but it does not explicitly link spectral properties to Hessian conditioning. Our proposal extends this by incorporating curvature-aware diagnostics, replacing heuristic fragility claims with formalized stability radii derived from Hessian condition numbers.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference promises significant computational savings but remains bottlenecked by heuristic calibration and static profiling. We propose Curvature-Aware Precision Routing (CAPR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on deterministic spectral and curvature proxies. By leveraging the spectral decay rate and Hessian condition number of hidden states, CAPR identifies tokens robust to low-precision computation and those requiring higher precision. We compute these diagnostics on-the-fly using randomized SVD and Hessian-vector products, mapping them to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. Theoretical PAC-Bayes bounds link spectral conditioning and Hessian stability to generalization robustness under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, CAPR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines. CAPR provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Diagnostic Validation: Measure the correlation between spectral decay rates, Hessian condition numbers, and quantization sensitivity across diverse datasets using randomized SVD and Hessian-vector products.
Routing Policy Evaluation: Compare CAPR to static mixed-precision baselines and Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) on WILDS benchmarks, evaluating throughput, accuracy, and latency.
Ablation Studies: Assess the individual contributions of spectral decay and Hessian conditioning to routing decisions, isolating their impact on computational efficiency and generalization stability.
PAC-Bayes Verification: Compute formal PAC-Bayes bounds linking spectral and Hessian diagnostics to generalization robustness under quantization noise, validating theoretical claims.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Computational Overhead: On-the-fly Hessian-vector products may introduce latency, though we mitigate this by caching diagnostics across tokens.
Generalization to Non-NLP Tasks: While validated on WILDS benchmarks, CAPR’s efficacy in vision or speech tasks remains untested.
Diagnostic Sensitivity: The accuracy of spectral decay and Hessian conditioning proxies may degrade under extreme distribution shifts.
